specification.topic-statement: The inter-agent messaging subsystem enables agents to send messages to one another, wait for incoming messages from specific senders, and terminate named agents, using the database as the shared communication medium.

scope.in-scope: The send operation (message delivery and database notification), the wait operation in both next-message and fan-in modes, the mailbox data model, database notification wakeup mechanism, message deletion after receipt, and the kill operation (agent termination).
scope.boundary: How send, wait, and kill are invoked as LLM tool calls is covered by the tool execution specification.
The state transitions that occur when an agent is killed or completes a wait are covered by the agent state machine specification.
How mailbox records are stored and queried in PostgreSQL is managed by the database layer and is out of scope here.

field.name: recipient agent UUID
field.note: the agent to whom the message is addressed

field.name: sender agent UUID
field.note: the agent who sent the message

field.name: session ID
field.note: the database session in which the message was written

field.name: target agent UUID
field.note: one of the agents being waited on

field.name: target agent name
field.note: human-readable name of the target agent

field.name: status
field.note: one of: running (still waiting), received (message arrived), idle (agent finished without sending), dead (agent was killed)

field.name: message body
field.note: the received message body; present only when status is received

behavior.send-operation:
The send operation delivers a message from the calling agent to a named recipient:
1. The recipient is validated: it must exist in the database and be currently marked as running. 2. The message is inserted into the database mailbox table with the sender's UUID, the recipient's UUID, and the body text. 3. If the database is not currently inside a transaction, a PostgreSQL NOTIFY event is fired on a channel named after the recipient's UUID. This wakes any agent currently blocking in a wait operation.
The operation returns immediately without waiting for the recipient to read the message. Messages accumulate in the mailbox until the recipient retrieves them.
PostgreSQL NOTIFY cannot be sent within a transaction (it would be rolled back if the transaction aborts). When a send occurs during a transaction, the notification is silently omitted. The recipient will discover the message on its next polling interval rather than being woken immediately.
behavior.wait-operation:
The wait operation suspends the calling agent until a message arrives or a timeout expires. Two modes are supported:
In next-message mode, the agent waits for any incoming message:
1. A LISTEN is registered on the database notification channel for this agent's UUID. 2. The mailbox is checked immediately; if a message is already present, it is returned without entering the polling loop. 3. A polling loop runs with 50-millisecond intervals. On each iteration: - Any pending database notifications are consumed. - The mailbox is queried for new messages. - If a message is found, it is returned (and deleted from the mailbox). - If an interrupt is detected, the wait exits with an interrupted status. - If the timeout has elapsed, the wait exits with a timeout status. 4. The LISTEN registration is removed before returning.
In fan-in mode, the agent waits for responses from a set of named target agents:
1. A LISTEN is registered on this agent's UUID channel and on each target's UUID channel. 2. A result table is initialized with one entry per target, all in running state. 3. Agent names are fetched from the database for all targets. 4. A polling loop runs with 50-millisecond intervals. On each iteration, for each target still in running state: - The mailbox is checked for a message from that target. - The target's database record is checked to determine if it has become idle or dead since the last check. - The result entry is updated accordingly. 5. The loop exits when all targets are resolved or the timeout expires. 6. All LISTEN registrations are removed before returning.
Each target's final state is one of: received (a message arrived), idle (the agent finished naturally without sending), or dead (the agent was killed).
Fan-in does not only detect message arrival — it also detects when a target agent transitions to idle or dead, allowing the waiting agent to learn that a worker has finished even if it sent no message.
Messages are discovered on the next polling check after the notification or state change occurs. Maximum additional latency before discovery is 50 milliseconds.
behavior.message-deletion: Messages are deleted from the mailbox immediately after being read by their recipient. Each retrieved message is not re-delivered on subsequent wait calls.
behavior.kill-operation:
The kill operation marks a target agent as dead in the database:
1. The target is validated: it must exist and be running. 2. Two structural constraints are enforced: - The root agent (the agent with no parent) cannot be killed. - An agent cannot kill its own parent. 3. The target is marked as dead in the database. 4. The agent's UUID is stored for post-completion processing. 5. After the tool call completes, the in-memory agent array is updated: any agent matching the killed UUID has its dead flag set.
Only the directly named target is marked dead. Child agents of the killed agent are not automatically killed. They continue to run until explicitly killed or until they exit naturally.
